text_content:
Most <1m pythons are great for beginners because they don’t see you as food. Pythons usually use a wrap and strangle method to kill prey, and that means if they don’t see you as viable food, they won’t try to hurt you for the most part. 

Just remember, snakes have their own temperament, just like any other animal. If your snake prefers not to be handled very much or something like that, don’t torture it. I know snake owners who don’t ever interact with their snake other than to feed and change water because the snake gets stressed out. Pythons are known as big one brain cell sweeties, but not all of them are comfortable with always being around you or out of the enclosure much.

video_title: Best beginners pet #snakes 🐍
text_content: In 1993 my dad got a berm. Never had a snake before. He ended up building a big enclosure for her in the garage and had her for 19 years. Never saw him cry for a cat or dog but he cried for her.

video_title: Best beginners pet #snakes 🐍
text_content: Oh. I was thinking king cobra, inland taipan, and death adder.

video_title: Best beginners pet #snakes 🐍
text_content:
Things people don’t tell you about ball pythons is how absolutely picky they can be. A single degree of difference in temperature or a different colored rat than usual and they’ll just straight up stop eating and think your trying poison them. They’re very dramatic at times but they are also incredibly sweet, just take that as warning! 

Source: I own a very dramatic ball python :,)

video_title: Best beginners pet #snakes 🐍
text_content: If your in for the long haul and want a big snake get a BCI boa. They get 7 footish but if you get a baby by the time it’s any kinda big you will know what your doing and they are some of the calmest most chilled out snakes hands down.

video_title: Best beginners pet #snakes 🐍
text_content: After owning several snakes, I definitely agree with these picks. The smallest is the corn snake, which is very easy to manage, and most pet shops will recommend them for first-time people. When I moved, the place didn't allow pets, so I had to surrender my bumblebee royal python. She was around 3 feet in length. Luckily, a pet shop owner took her and actually still has her. She's bigger now, but still the sweet nature snake I remember.

text_content: Ball Pythons have a certain morph they can get that is associated with a neurological condition called the "wobble," which is kind of like an unsteadiness in the head movements. Just smth to look out for ^^ They look like their markings are spider webs, very cool morphs.

text_content: I used to be be TERRIFIED of snakes. Still can be if they aren’t a pet and even then. My high school had a teacher that had snakes and insects and such in his classroom (I think he was a science teacher so it worked) and let students pet/ hold the non-dangerous ones so that they wouldn’t be scared of the snakes anymore. Anyway he’d just found this BRIGHT orange corn snake in the woods by his house and took it in. He said it had to have been dumped because that color would make it a target in the wild. My friend suggested I hold the snake and I did. I was terrified at first but then she was just kind of chilling on me and slowly climbing up my arm. It took a lot of the fear away because she was acting friendly and silly. She tried to get into my shirt sleeve lol. I think I’m fine with it if they are harmless snakes that are pets. In the wild I will still have a freak out about a snake though.

video_title: Corn Snake vs Ball Python: What Makes A Better Pet? 🤔🐍

text_content: Ball Python, personally. Mine has his own personality and they can even be trained to only have a feeding response when you tap on the enclosure with the tongs. He transitioned from mice to rats effortlessly. I only had one instance where he went off food, but it was related to him developing minor scale rot and the stress of the treatment caused him to not care for eating. He went off food for 5 months till he completely healed, but didn't loose any weight in that time period. I've had him for three years and he's one of the best things that ever happened to me. Absolute goofball.

text_content: My whole life I’ve wanted a snake but I’ve always been worried about certain things or I guess misconceptions about snakes now that I’ve been watching your videos. video_title: Ball Python, The Best Pet Reptile?
video_description: Clint talks specifically about the ball python (Python regius), weighing the merits of these incredible snakes so you can decide if ...
text_content:
My son had a ball python until the end of her life...he’s an adult now, he and his friends grew up with her as one of the gang and she won over countless snake haters...he had friends who were literally afraid to come into our home they were so scared of snakes and before long they were hanging out with her too. They wore her on their heads like crowns, watched tv with her in their laps, and she was overall, the very best, quietest, cleanest, pet we ever had. She never ever even so much as acted like she would bite or be aggressive and I’m sure at times they were annoying little
boys. She was curious and seemed to truly enjoy being with them. She has a place of honor buried beneath our Spirea bushes. Best pet ever!

text_content:
A BP was my first snake, and I can confidently say they're a fantastic first reptile. They're tough and very capable of handling small mistakes that come from beginner snake ownership - 

I struggled with humidity for the first few sheds, but now I stick some warm, wet moss in the cold hide as soon as his eyes go blue and he sheds perfectly every time. Fresh substrate change and deep clean after every shed, with poop and urate collection inbetween. 

Temperature is something I've struggled with, but now I'm running two heat mats in the tank hooked up to one thermostat. SInce one heat mat is less powerful, that ends up being the cold end at around 80f, and the hot end sits at 92f. Ambient temps drop at night to around 75f in the cold end, but the actual temp inside the cold hide is still fine at 80. He seems to prefer cold end over hot one, despite me playing with temps to see if I had it too high, he just prefers one end over the other. No biggie, he's perfectly healthy

Feeding's always been easy thankfully. I rehomed him from someone who was keeping him in a tiny tub and only feeding mice, when she should've been on large weaner rats at the time. He's now just over 4ft long and eating small adult rats every two weeks or so, and his weight and body condition is steady. I can see when he's hungry again, cause he lays out in the viv with his head perched up on a log, and that's the sign he wants to eat again. He had very lazy strikes when I first got him, but now he explodes for food, so all good there. He missed about 3 feeds in the two years I've had him. Never worked out why,  just seems like they do that some times. Temperature of the rat is vital to getting a good feeding response from him, as BPs have heat sensing organs on their face - if the rat doesn't have the temperature of a live one, he takes much longer to decide to eat it. 90-95f seems ideal for feeding consistently. Also helps a lot to feed him in the dark at night. Not total darkness or he misses the food, but a dim, distant light being on somewhere in the room seems ideal.

His viv is 4ftx18x24in and he's happy in it. It has three hides, a water bowl, some wood and fake plants. He likes climbing sometimes, so I'd recommend some bits for them to climb a bit. He was small when I got him, so he outgrew his 3ft viv within a year, and has been through 3 different sizes of hides.


*If you're looking at getting one as a first snake, go for it. I'd recommend a temperature gun as one of the most useful tools for beginners - it allows you to easily check the snake's body temp, the temp in all areas of the viv, the room temps, the food temps. A fantastic tool for putting your mind at ease, and you can pick them up for like $10*

text_content:
I've had my ball python for almost a year now and there's some stuff I wish I'd have known before hand! I did a tonne of research but ultimately if you decide you're equipped to keep one well then you just have to go for it and learn through physical experience. 

The main thing I wish I'd known is how quickly they grow. I got my guy when he was 6 months old, and in less than a year he's pretty much outgrown his tank and now I'm in the market for a new one. I shouldn't have wasted my money on a smaller "grow out" viv! Big vivs are expensive and take up a lot of space, and 100% necessary. 

They are sensitive, if husbandry is off or if they are sick they won't eat. A lot of people put missed meals down to "typical" BP behavior rather than checking their snake's home and parameters, or getting them checked by a reptile vet. Yes some do go off food, but its much safer to not assume and investigate everything before assuming its normal! 

They are expensive because of their size - the current cost of living crisis here is causing a lot of people to abandon their pets, snakes included. Make sure you have the resources to cover every eventuality before you take one in! (that goes for every pet tbh)

I love my pal, and don't regret him at all. I'm looking forward to a hopefully long and healthy life with him! But I feel sometimes reptiles fall victim to the same treatment as fish, people think you can buy a tank and just throw them in and not bother with them and provide 0 effort. I'm glad these kind of videos exist and that we're finally changing attitudes towards their welfare!

text_content:
I'm not saying the stepping stone thing is wrong or anything, because I do agree, I just want to make a comment.

It's not just about if you think you can care for the reptile, but also the money. Snakes are extremely expensive, buying the snake, the enclosures, beddings, food, lights, tank accessories, and more. Not to be a discouragement, though, a 40 gal can be around 200 dollars, but then you can get a used one for 50. You just have to work your way around it.

I was unfortunately not allowed to have the snake I wanted for my first (red-tailed boa) and got a ball python instead. What I didn't know was that this ball python would be one of the most special pets I have ever had, and she was just an amazing little snake. She was so friendly with people, had perfect sheds, never had eating problems (except when I first got her), and just the sweetest, most calm soul ever. Today, I now have 4 ball pythons, and I love them all.

text_content: For some time ball pythons weren’t really a species I was into, because of how common they were, and the food response. So instead I went for other species that have similar advantages, but with better feeding responses like my python curtus and candoia paulsoni tasmai. Then one day I discovered the banana pied morph, and I later came across the green room python’s channel which slowly changed my view on them until I decided to go for one, a banana pied at that. The feeding response was indeed an issue, I couldn’t figure out what would make him eat, it felt a bit random, sometimes it worked other times it didn’t, I was this close to give up on him, until I finally found it, and now he’s eating consistently. But I gotta say though, apart from that he is a fantastic snake, whenever I take him out he is very curious and very active, he will explore every inch of the room, and will even come to me from time to time. I ended up getting one when I felt I was ready and actually wanted one, and I am so glad I did. But they definitely aren’t for everyone, that feeding response was nerve racking and many rats were wasted in the process, so as for all animals, make sure you’re ready for them.

text_content: Before anything, massive kudos to this man for being an incredible pet owner and parent. While this is a super touching video and I absolutely adore snakes, it doesn’t always go this way. Far too many people who have no business owning these creatures do and I’m sure just as many have children in the house. Not saying this to deter anyone from keeping snakes, they can be wonderful pets but you have to understand this man has put A LOT of work into socializing this Python and that alone isn’t easy. There is a balance between handling, socializing and stressing a snake out. I’m no biologist but as a keeper everything  he has said in this video is textbook how to raise a snake. Hard work? Absolutely but look at the end result. He has given that snake a wonderful life where it can thrive and most importantly his daughter experiences most children will never have. I’m sure this has taught her to be kind and respectful of things beyond her years. Beautiful all around.

text_content:
I used to have a 14ft Burmese that I got when he was around 10ft long. It was during the winter (he was a birthday gift) and the reptile store we got him from was going out of business because of the horrible way they ran the store. It was *freezing* in that store, and the snake was only like 150 dollars, which for a 10ft Burmese is mindblowing. But they needed to sell him and most others were intimidated by his size. I had them take him out of the enclosure he was in and he seemed fairly calm, but I learned a few days later it was only because he was too cold to show his aggression. For about 3 months he'd strike at his enclosure when we walked by, we had to cover his head to change his water and clean the pen, and feeding him was a lesson in stress.

Then I started letting him out of his pen and it was like night and day. The more he was out and socialized, the better he got at being handled and around people in general. Eventually he got to where he would actively seek out company with us, he'd get into the shower with us, he *loved* attention from the neighborhood kids when we'd take him out to get direct sunlight during the spring and summer. He never once even hissed at the kids, let alone tried to bite them. He basically became a giant scaly dog. People think reptiles incapable of any kind of emotional connection but they really aren't these cold heartless unfeeling machines most people believe them to be.

text_content:
Oh, I must say that *THIS* is the video I have been yearning for, for the longest time!  Even though I can't see myself ever personally owning a reticulated python, it is absolutely one of my favorite snake species 😍 so when I realized "Clint made a retic video!" it was a moment of unabashed excitement for me!

When I see some of the largest specimens alongside a human, I can only think to myself, "Dear gods, that is a *LOT* of snake!"  (Kind of like my reaction to the green anaconda in that other video.)  It's kind of like the analogy of "the moth and the flame" -- where I'm drawn to it for being so large, powerful, and impressive, but it also represents a potential safety hazard, on account of those very same characteristics.  Then again, you could say the same thing about lions, tigers, wolves, etc., and people love those.

My eventual goal is to see (and interact with) a retic someday at a reptile facility or event, under the supervision of experienced keepers.  One of the Earth's natural treasures, to be loved, valued, and treated with respect! 🐍

video_title: My Top 10 Reasons Ball Pythons Make Great Pets #ballpython #royalpython #reptiles #reptile
text_content:
Got my first 2 snakes last year for my birthday, I’ve always wanted one. My dad had bought them from a pet store and I didn’t realize until a like 3 weeks later they hate mites. My heart dropped, I didn’t know anything about mites and I looked it up and tried treating them myself and quarantined them separately for about 1 almost 2 months while using mite spray on them once a day. I stopped seeing the mites so I took them off of quarantine. They seemed perfectly fine but maybe about a week later, I found lots of mites in their tank (which I thoroughly cleaned before I let them out of quarantine) I did more researches and I found out they had a respiratory infection I’m not sure how they got it since their tank was always clean. Me and my parents search around but there was no exotic vet near us, the ones that were, were closed. I was basically balling my eyes out for days (I’m tearing up as I type this) they sadly passed away. A month later, I bought a snake from a reptile expo. I have given her new things and threw out all of the old supplies I’ve had and cleaned very well before her arrival and also brought a new tank (at the reptile expo, I was meant to buy my old snakes 2 tanks before they were gone) but now I am happy with my ball python who will be turning 1 year next month, she’s very healthy and happy and everything is going good, I’d clip a picture of her if I could.

Moral of the story,
Don’t buy reptiles from a pet store, they only want your money and probably don’t know the first thing to care for them… they didn’t even know my snakes age or gender which was the first red flags 🚩🚩

video_title: My Top 10 Reasons Ball Pythons Make Great Pets #ballpython #royalpython #reptiles #reptile
text_content:
Our Ivory girl, Luna, has been a joy to have.  We were told she was vicious and unpredictable, and that she would only eat live.  Well, she’s absolutely covered in scars from that kind of thinking.  She took a f/t rat her first week with us, and has never struck at us.  She hisses sometimes if we annoy her.  Lol

My son has autism and she has helped him,  for sure be more responsible, but also to come out of his shell and improve his speech, dexterity, and reflexes.  It was a pleasant surprise.  🥰

text_content: This is the cutest video ever omg. I think it’s so important when parents teach their young kids to both respect and love animals that’s aren’t just mammals. This girl will never have the typical bias or unwarranted hatred towards snakes that so many ppl do and learning to build a bond with a creature as different from us as a snake will make her more empathetic towards ALL creatures later in life. Snakes get so much hate that just isn’t deserved and while I understand phobias aren’t always rational, most snakes (at least here in the US) are relatively harmless. Even in regards to wild snakes(both venomous and non venomous), as long as you respect the animal via leaving it be and keeping your distance, it won’t usually harm you. Domestic snakes are generally kept as pets BC of their docile nature and you could do a lot more harm to them than the other way around. Kids are far more likely to be attacked by a dog than a pet snake and dogs are also able to cause way more severe injuries than a snake of this size (where the largest risk is simply infection). Yet bc of this stigma against them SO many snakes are murdered or even tortured by humans every day when the snake is simply minding its own business and wants to be left alone. We, as a society, need to learn to stop fearing or hating anything that’s different from us (both people and other animals) and teaching them at a young age to have compassion for an animal so misunderstood as a snake is the best way to do it imo. I think this video also shows the incredible bond snakes can have with humans despite us being so different and that they’re capable of love as well 💗

text_content: These comments really goes to show that it's basically impossible to remove the stigma people have of snakes no matter what. Those snakes are not big enough to eat humans, they don't leave them unattended with their children, and snakes don't "size up" their prey they know exactly how big of an animal they can eat and thats about the size of a large rabbit - it's controlled by the gap in their skull for their throat, and they can't eat anything bigger than that. And no, they can't "unhinge" their jaws to increase that

text_content: I think one of the main things people don’t understand is that snakes have personalities just like a cat or dog does. They think and they feel and they are very intelligent!! We got my grandson a ball python and my granddaughter has played with it more than her brother has. We have had him for years and that snake has never even struck at any of us!! Not one time have I ever felt iffy about this snake! He crawls all over my granddaughter and has since she was 4. I mean she knows how to respect him too! You’ve got to teach that too. Our snake feels secure that I won’t let anyone mistreat him and I take that very seriously! Honestly, their quirks are so fun!! He can be in one of his hides but if he hears my voice he’ll peek his head out and see what I’m doing! I know I’ve gone on and on but I just can’t stress enough what amazing pets snakes make!!❤

text_content: Dog bites and maulings are common because of the nature of how common they are but also how difficult. Cats and dogs are harder to keep than snakes. Dogs require tons of supervision, training, teaching your kids how to appropriately interact, and need to eat twice a day and go on walks and be potty trained. A snake literally poops just once a week, eats once a week, and isn't needy. Dogs can be awesome but just not for toddlers unless you're a very attentive owner with a lot of time or training funds on your hands.

text_content: Having had two a literal rattlesnakes in the past, I recommend going with the biggest a literal rattlesnake you can find. The big a literal rattlesnakes are full of bluster, but rarely a problem. However, the small a literal rattlesnakes are much more bite than bark. I had a pretty big Mojave Green that, while outwardly aggressive, never made an actual bite attempt. The little sidewinder I don’t think I heard rattle more than a few times, but she would strike at the drop of a hat and was mind-bogglingly fast. I took them both seriously, but I always kept extra on guard any time I had to handle the little’un.

video_description: Thanks for stopping by, Subscribe and like Patreon : https://www.patreon.com/Ben_cincysnakes Instagram : Ben CincySnakes ...
text_content:
Beautiful burm!  I love Burmese pythons. Its a shame that what people see on the news about Florida is all that most people will ever know about them. 

Theyve been a favorite of mine since i was a kid. Long story short, I wanted an African rock python for my 10th birthday (i had been keeping ball pythons and other snakes for years) but got a Burmese python instead. 

Now im in my mid 30s and keep Retics and Afrocks too. They are all so different but i love them all. 

Back in the day Burms were easily the most popular of the 3, but holy cow have Retics taken over lately! Hardly anyone keeps Burms and even fewer keep Afrocks these days.

 IMO Burms are the most practical for most people. Although they are more prone to respiratory issues. 
Afrocks are hardier for sure, but require a better understanding of snake behavior when it comes to handling them. They are NOT mean, but they are less forgiving than burms or retics on average. If you put the time in though, they are the most interesting to work with by far. Even smarter than retics believe it or not! more powerful and athletic too! At least lb for lb.

text_content: THANK YOU SO MUCH for this Very educational video! I own two Reticulated Pythons, they are terrible pets for 99% of pet owners,  Reticulated Pythons and Burma Pythons are only experienced snake owners with 4+ years of owning snakes already. I love Burmese pythons with all my heart and soul . They are lovely looking animals and astonishing to look at when full grown but they are terrible pets just like Asian Water Monitors. Good Video! I love how you stressed the cons and told people the complete truth. I own two Reticulated Pythons and I DO NOT recommend them at all as house pets. Reticulated Pythons are normally aggressive snakes, they eat a lot , they poop a lot, they average 12 feet long for males and females CAN grow up to 25 feet long rarely. Females usually 17 - 20 feet long. 300+ pounds has occurred it's possible for female Burma Pythons, African Rock Pythons and Sumatran  Reticulated Pythons. They are GIGANTIC! The food bill is though the roof and they can kill you easily when in food mode once they are at least 8 feet long. That being said. I adore Reticulated Pythons and love my male/female pair of Reticulated Pythons. The 10 foot long plastic cage that an adult female retic needs is expensive. At least $900 for the adult enclosure. Same with burms.

text_content:
I absolutely love berms, the ones I've known who have been handled were all sweethearts, and I've helped people get pretty decent sized ones (15ish foot was the largest berm) get used to being held. Even if they start off highly defensive (more helpful to keep in mind when a snake strikes it's a defensive act rather than aggressive one in most cases unless they honestly think your body part is food), I've found with daily work and proper care they tend to chill out relatively quickly.

The hardest one I helped a friend work with was her slightly larger reticulated python her son never touched then abandoned with her. She couldn't handle him but wanted to do her best to keep him 'cause she felt it wasn't his fault. His enclosure wasn't the best size, but once he calmed down he was usually chilling outside it.

I may love the actual giant snakes, but after a certain point size becomes the limiter, but it isn't just for size and danger level the way many think, after a certain size their feed animals get tricky. I mean- you can go to meat markets and farms to get prekilled animals telling them not to butcher the meat for you, but it gets tricky depending on where you live... even when they don't eat nearly as often as smaller snakes.

I wouldn't want to get an animal who need to eat feed animals larger than a rat, ideally frozen/thawed- and I feel bad about that 'cause I love rats as pets... unless it was fish. Far easier to go to a fish market to get whole fish of varying sizes if you're anywhere near port cities. SO easy.

So yeah- I'd sooner have an adult alligator where they can eat a wider range of meats and I wouldn't have to feed whole mammals and whole chickens consistently- though as any other reptile they need a varied diet, than a reticulated python- but to be fair, I wouldn't have alligators either.


Though I have known someone with a young alligator and "he" was awesome- so the temptation when I was told one could be gotten for me if I wanted was greater than when I was offered a breeding pair of anacondas for free.

Though won't lie- was actually tempted at the idea of them, my brain went to turning my basement apartment into a giant snake room with the sizable bedroom turned into a pool with a waterfall for them... then I thought "Nah- I really shouldn't own anything that could kill me..." and turned them down.


Edited to add- yeah- that part with you telling people what to do is needed if you aren't with people who know- when I helped with larger snakes where it goes into "I may not die but my ribs are breaking if not other bones..." mode and up I just have an open bottle of Listerine or a strong drinking alcohol around in case something happened and I get incapacitated and seriously have seconds to get it off- I think what people forget or don't think of is every time you breathe out they constrict more, so when you try to breathe in again you can't and so on until... ugh- I hate the idea of hurting a snake, but alcohol is one of the "easier" ways with a high likelyhood of success of getting a snake disengaged- that and grabbing the tail and pulling back can make continued constricting much harder for them, if not impossible- depending on the size.

Very very intentionally I've topped out my handling of the large snakes at 15 feet, I want 2 people with me at a min, esp if I know they're quirky- the alcohol of the non-rubbing kind uncapped and ready for someone to grab, and a phone close enough so 911 can be called. Only a few times I only had 1 other person, and a few times whoever was with me wasn't experienced. I have no interested with working with larger on any kind of regular basis...

That brings back memories. I had to surrender my pet snake when we were starting our family. Not something i wanted around with a small child. Tabby looked just like this but she was well over 15 ft in length and much thicker. She was so gentle as long as you took the proper scent precautions. I miss her but i can still see her at the zoo that took her in due to her being Albino and in such great condition. Everyone loves the Albino ones for how they look.

100% not the type of animal you want as a pet. They require a ton of care and eventually they will become so large that it's impractical to keep and likely a danger.

text_content: Honestly I’d argue giant constrictors (with the exception of green anacondas where the whole semiaquatic issue renders their enclosure not only big but also hard to maintain) are the most reasonable of unreasonable pet reptiles. Certainly far saner than most venomous snakes or any crocodilian. The main issue with giant constrictors is just enclosure size and everything associated with maintaining such a big enclosure, while venomous snakes are a lot more likely to result in injury or death and crocodilians (and some monitors) need even bigger enclosures plus a more complicated setup.

video_title: Do Burmese Pythons make good pets?
video_description: Meet Banana Meringue, our albino Burmese Python! In this week's video, Casey demonstrates why you might not want a giant ...
text_content:
What do you mean Burmese pythons don’t make good pets?
I was on board with you up until you said that.
That is absolutely false information sir.
Yes they are big snakes, yes some people shouldn’t own them if they are small. And don’t mean that because they will be eaten I mean that because it’s a heavy snake. Now, if you’re 4’8” and 75lb soakin wet ya you could be mistaken for prey long enough it could be bad.
But a full grown average sized human is perfectly capable of handling these animals.

For anyone reading this that doesn’t know a lot about Burmese, please understand that these snakes are known to be one of the most docile big snakes that there are. You can literally pet their head and it will watch you and be perfectly fine with it. They are super intelligent and you can see it.

If you like snakes, and wouldn’t mind a big snake, a Burmese python is a great choice.

Don’t let the stuff in Florida scare you. People there will take 5 others with them and jump on big snakes grabbing their head. What do expect the snakes reaction to be? Duh..
You can’t blame a snake, it’s not their fault what they do they are truly creatures of instinct.

text_content: My only concern with recommending anyone get a burmese python is the amount of space required for their enclosure. Yeah, you're right that the actual care isn't too hard, but I don't think most people understand or are able to supply the vast amount of space needed for them to live comfortably and thrive. We're talking like 4 ft by 8 ft floor space at the least for an adult. They can be such wonderful and active snakes when given the room they need, but often I see them housed in small tubs or small cages where they can really only have enough room to coil up and sit in a corner all day. I just hope you can provide the space needed and I'm hoping your new place provides that space. :)

video_title: HUGE SNAKE | Burmese Python | My Biggest Pet Snake | Creature Feature
video_description: Slither on over to http://audible.com/emzotic for your FREE 30 day trial! HUGE python - the biggest snake I have. CREATURE ...
text_content:
Hey Creatures!! Welcome to the first of my Halloween Creature Features! *Hissclaimer* Usually I would advise not to handle your snake during shed, but Bess did the most gargantuan poop, and I had to bring him out for cleaning anyway and I took the chance to shoot this video with him to show the masses how amazing their eyes look when in shed. Bess's eyes will always look blue, even when he's not in shed, because hes exceptionally old, and this old man has cataracts in both eyes. 
Anyhow, I know this message won't deter the masses from going 'OMG HES IN SHED - LEAVE HIM ALOOOOOOOONE', but I've said my piece about this and Bess has now happily shed his skin and is currently chowing down on his lunch. He's so sweet and wonderful, and he's one of my rescues. He's been with me since January this year. :) 
Thanks everyone for being here. Please feel free to share this video far and wide!!

text_content:
I have a true story about a snake. I'm italian so this will be hard for me to tell in english, but I'll try. 
My beloved grandfather fought in ww2, but he was captured and he sadly had to spent seven years of his life as a prisoner of war in South Africa. They were in a camp and a certain day they woke up and they were told that they had to move. He had an old folded piece of fabric as a pillow and when he lifted it up he found out there was a viper under it. He had probably spent the night with this lethal snake under his head. Everybody wanted to kill the animal, but my grandaddy told them that they should let him live and go, just like the snake did with him... I was a little girl but I remember how proud of him I was... still miss him every day.

video_description: Twitch Livestreams! http://twitch.tv/newenglandreptile http://twitch.tv/donnyrapture ⭐️10% off frozen food! RodentPro.com Code: ...
text_content: I have kept king cobras, retics, and Burmese pythons, and will have to agree on the first two's intelligence level. Kings don't go into spastic mode like many other cobras or venomous snakes do. They observe and measure you , appearing to judge your intentions based on what or how you are moving. One has to keep these animals to fully understand what I am talking about here.  Retics, on the other hand, are inquisitive; sometimes when you open up their cage and see their heads turning and tongue flicking in your direction, you'd think that a bite would be forthcoming once you pick them up, as that is what usually happens with a lot of other snakes. You don't really see that behavior in Burms unless they are anticipating food.  Also with the Burmese, you can start picking them up immediately upon opening the enclosure, but with most retics, you will have to feel them first and try to gauge their mood before reaching out.  Not saying that retics are more predisposed to aggression, it's just that they go through mood swings far more frequently than the Burmese.

text_content: I'd rather separate a hatched clutch of burms or retics than a clutch of corn snakes lol. Burms and retics are actually more mindful of when and where they're biting so you can be more careful. Not that bites really hurt as hatchlings from either species  but fucking corns just mindlessly bite lmao. I've had three biting me on one finger and one of the ones biting me was getting gnawed on by it's sibiling. Plus opening up a box of baby burms or retics hissing is actually impressive cuz for being so small it's still loud lol. Some corns make almost like puffing noises when they're hissing, some sound like they're hyperventilating, and other sound like they're running out of oxygen and going to die XD. Don't me me wrong I love all forms of life and respect all forms, I even have an anery, butter stripe and a reverse oketee corn. All I'm saying for the most part a lot of baby snakes can and will have simmilar attitudes even if they're different species.none of them have a relationship or even experience with a human so. Reptiles are really fun and rewarding but if you're scared of being bitten then it's probably not for you. That's not to say that every or potentially even any reptile will even bite you but it's possible. Snakes mainly you should just plan to be bitten. That's not meaning  by every snake you ever handle I mean that as in if you've had the chance to handle more than probably 7-8 snakes you've probably been bit or at least struck at.

text_content: I have my doubts that a 20ft burm ever existed tbh. Even "Baby" from serpent safari in wisconsin dells wasnt that big, and shes one of the biggest burms whose ever lived, at a confirmed 400lbs. I saw her in person ages ago, aswell as medusa the retic. Both were morbidly obese, but baby was definitely under 20ft. Medusa was a freak though. Id bet my life that she was easily over 25ft when i saw her. She was a retic though of course.  I also worked with an 18.5ft burmese at arizona reptile center and i can see why people might assume shes 20ft or more. Anyways thats just my opinion. Maybe there are real 20ft burms out there, but i have doubts. I think that if someone told me they had a 20ft snake, id assume they were full of it, unless it was a female retic.

text_content:
I 100% agree with getting rubbed the wrong way when people are like "I'm going to buy it. But can always sell it later on if it gets too big for me to handle." Just like people who get a puppy and then 10 yrs later they're like "Oh, it's old and needs all this special care that I don't want to deal with" and drop it off at a shelter. I feel like any animal you bring in becomes a family member, and is a family member for life. It's not like you can go "oh, my son is failing all his classes and does nothing but get in trouble. I don't want to deal with it anymore. I'll just get rid of him." You can't do that to your human family members, and shouldn't do that to animals you bring into your household.

Also agree with not settling for a snake you don't want. I wanted a bullsnake as my first pet snake. But when I went to my first reptile show, no vendors there were selling bullsnakes. I know I should have just gone home with no snake. But after spending months researching Hong different species and which ones I wanted, I didn't want to wait any longer. I did like ball pythons, so I found one I really liked. He's now my problem child, constantly going on feeding strikes. lol. He's also my pet rock that does nothing but hide.

text_content:
This is spot on. Fussing too much over what would make a good beginner snake will likely lead you to end up with an animal you don't want. 

Its better to spend a little bit longer, waiting until you've researched the crap out of a more intermediate animal and waiting until you are ready for the animal you want, then settling for an animal you dont want.

Personally I'm happy with my little ball python, but I've rescued all my animals. And most of these snakes wouldnt have to end up being rescued if people didnt make rash choices in what they buy.

text_content: Had an albino Burmese Python many years ago; she was absolutely beautiful and my God what a big baby. She absolutely loved being around people, she would slither over to the couch and come up to lay with you. I eventually married, had children, and had to move to a location which made it difficult to buy food for her; this was prior to the internet so I couldn’t just order it like today. I reluctantly gave her to a friend of mine who was a veterinarian. He raised alligators, iguanas, and tegus so she was in great hands. My wife, daughter, and I would go visit her regularly.  He kept her for many years and even bred her before she finally passed away. She was a beast, it generally took two of us to move her around for habitat renovations, etc.

text_content: I LOVE the contact to the snake before you began handling it. Not all kept snakes are this friendly, but snakes that tend to strike when you first open their enclosure likely think they're getting fed. Many snake owners will gently tap or touch their snake before handling it to let them know that it is handling time and not feeding time. This way, you will have fewer chances of getting mistaken for a meal when spending time with your beautiful noodle :)

text_content: I wish more people would give Carpet Pythons the time of day.  Irian Jaya Carpet pythons are basically as hardy as a corn snake and top out around 6ft. That was my wifes 1st snake 9 years ago and now she keeps retics and all sorts of stuff. Anyways, IJ carpet pythons have surprisingly low humidity requirements compared to other members of Morelia. They also do not need it particularly hot. Low to Mid 80s is plenty for the warm side. Unlike some carpet python localities they only really need to be misted if they are shedding. My male hasnt skipped a meal in 9 years. One of the easiest snakes i own. Even my ball python technically takes more work because i have to mist her and she likes to skip meals. It is probably true that it is harder to get bitten by a ball python than a carpet python, but my Carpet python hasnt ever bitten anyone either. He has a fantastic feeding response, but as long as you use your head its easy to manage. He is tap trained and all that too. I mostly keep Burms, retics, and an Afrock. So hes kind of like the training snake since he acts very similarly to the retics.

text_content:
Pine snakes and gopher snakes are the best beginner snake to me. Their appetite is crazy, they are super active and more interesting to watch than corn snakes, their enclosure is pretty easy to build and maintain, they're very bold and inquisitive, and yes their defensive posture is impressive but also really cool and among all of my snakes, earning their trust was the most rewarding feeling.

The idea that they're mean and cranky really is a myth

text_content:
If I could afford & had the experience to keep any monitor top 3 hands down for me is 

1- lace monitor (I don’t have enough experience + they are so expensive to get in my area that I’d need a miracle to get them) 

2- blue tree monitor (pricy but not bad as lace issue is humidity since my area is dry & I know it would struggle, experience I’d still say I’m not ready for yet but am getting close) 

3- black roughneck (was thinking I might have just enough experience thanks to going crazy to research laces as I LOVE them but this video did kinda make a few points I didn’t think about at all to open my eyes a bit more lol + they aren’t easy to find for me it seems) 


Also I’ve never heard of varanus celebensis before how big do they get? If they don’t get over 5Ft & have the same laid back personality of its larger relative I might go for this as a first monitor instead of forced into a dwarf monitor as i really love monitors that HEAVILY resemble a Komodo dragon in the face & bulk hence why I loved laces

text_content:
If you are absolutely new to new-ish to reptile keeping and have a little money (probably $500USD for reptile and enclosure, etc), I'd recommend a Keel-bellied Lizard. Monitor-adjacent in looks and somewhat in temperament (a little more skittish). 
The tail might be a turnoff for some, and they do need some height and width, with some ways to move vertically and kind of diagonally. Mine would sometimes run around on the "ground" part of my enclosure, but not frequently.

text_content: I always wanted a beardie when I was young, but upon getting one as an adult I was not prepared for how cute, friendly and personable my new lizard companion would be. They are (dare I say) affectionate, fun, inquisitive, playful and entertaining. I was worried my two huge tabby barn cats would terrorize her, until on one particular occasion she chased one of them out of the room when the cat was stalking up on us, while we had our beardie out to play! We still don't have them directly interact, but they know each other and she is not seen as prey by our kitties so life is good. Our beardie loves eating grasshoppers, swimming and taking baths, going for walks/adventures out side, car rides and perching on a shoulder, but is always happy to return to her enclosure- which we have placed elevated in the main room so that she can observe the goings on in our home as we, our toddler (who she loves) and the cats go about our day. (toddler is not allowed to handle lizard, but she loves to watch him play, and expressed interest in BEFORE our cats

text_content:
please DO NOT get a reptile without knowing the time and care that goes into getting one.
Yes, they are pretty,
yes, they are 'adorable as babies' (that's why my mother ran a sanctuary that took in well over 200 unwanted reptiles)
yes, they are fun to look at.
but they're a commitment that require a lot more work than a dog or cat.
If you have the money, space, time, and knowledge- then yes! get one and enjoy!

video_title: My new Argus Monitor!
text_content: I had a savanna monitor. We have pictures of my grandma feeding Rex ham off of a fork and holding him like a baby. THEYRE SO AWESOME. And then we had Lola… a Nile River water monitor. She was only about six months or so old but the owner didn’t tell us for one, that she was a Nile River water monitor, but 2 that he didn’t handle her enough. Every single time we took her out she would latch onto my mom’s finger and never let go. Slowly as she got bigger, we had to put gloves on or else she’d hurt my mom. She never lost her aggressiveness so we had to stop holding her, but she’s still such a beauty.

text_content:
I actually interned at a zoo for awhile that kept this species.

Only reptile I’ve met that was possibly even friendlier than a bearded dragon. Our’s LOVED people, and reminded me of a giant puppy. Would follow you around the enclosure when you cleaned it in the hope you had food. Never tried to bite or attack anyone. Would come up to the front of the enclosure and sit there for hours because it liked to watch all the guests- and most of our mammals didn’t even do that. 

Wicked smart, one of the keepers did target training with it to make moving it/vet visits easier, and to keep it’s brain occupied.

BUT due to their enormous size there’s very few people I would ever recommend this to.
It’s one of those animals that basically needs it’s own room in your house, and honestly even that likely isn’t big enough. And that’s being conservative on space, not being one of those people that recommend huge enclosures for any random animal. Certain people/organizations can properly keep them and should be allowed to do so, but I wouldn’t casually recommend them to most people.

text_content: I had one of these in high school in the '70's.  I researched these as best I could before buying it, even to the point that I learned V. salvator was far superior to V. niloticus as pets.  Visited a biker in Fla. who had a 6 footer that he kept like a dog in an indoor kennel...spiked collar and all.  His lizard would come when called like a dog, and seemingly enjoyed being petted like a dog.  After that, I was hooked, and bought one a little bigger than the one in the video.  My dad built his enclosure.  He was literally like a cross between a dog, a cat and a dragon.  I enjoyed being his buddy for several years before he developed a respiratory infection.  In force-feeding him his medicine for that, I traumatized his mouth, resulting in his getting mouth rot which proved fatal.  This video brought tears to my eyes, remembering both Godzilla and my dad who helped me care for him.  Thank you.

text_content:
Here’s some random-ish facts about the Asian water monitor (and monitors in general.)
 Monitors have forked tongues, making them the only reptiles other than snakes to possess this characteristic. Like snakes, this tongue shape allows for better accuracy in locating a prey's scent.
Monitors practice 'open pursuit' hunting instead of stalking and ambushing. They are very fast despite their massive size because of their powerful leg muscles. When escaping predators, usually large snakes, water monitors will climb a tree using their powerful legs. If this technique doesn't work, they will jump from the branch into the safety of a stream or river.
Monitors are known to use their tail (which may be twice as long as its body) as a rudder (used to steer when swimming), for grasping, and as a weapon.
Monitors swallow their food whole or in large pieces; they are able to dislocate their thyroid bone in order to enlarge their throat.
Males are extremely territorial. Upon encountering another male they will first take a threatening posture then begin fighting viciously, often leaving severe bite wounds.
These monitors build their burrows along riverbanks and will take advantage of termite mounds as well as rotting logs or stumps to lay their eggs.
Asian water monitors can remain under water for up to 30 minutes - which comes in handy when hunting aquatic prey.
These water monitors are comfortable in both fresh and saltwater and have been spotted swimming far out in the ocean.

text_content: Hey Brian. I've watched your videos for years and this is my first time EVER making a comment on YouTube period. First I just want to thank you for all the content you and the whole crew provides. Seriously, THANK YOU. I also want to thank you for how active you are with fighting for our rights and promoting USARK (truthfully I don't support currently but will when my financial situation improves). This brings me to a question however, and please don't take this wrong. Why support Petsmart/Petco when they actively support organizations like HSUS? It seems counter intuitive 😕 I mean ultimately they are helping get rid of reptiles right? I don't want to pretend I know what I'm talking about because literally EVERYTHING I know is from your videos (and other hobbyists/educators on YouTube). I just don't understand driving people to these stores by giving them this advertising. I say this humbly and again thank you for all the education, encouragement, and entertainment you've provided.

text_content: I love snakes, aligators and monitors since in my childhood they are my lovely babies ❤ living with reptiles always giving me a unforgettable and a beautiful moments.

video_title: The best beginner pet lizard
text_content: We found a Beardy in the wilds of Florida when I was 13, abandoned by an owner most likely, and took him in. Absolutely the sweetest baby.. calm, easy to handle, loved to just be on you and watch the tv. Bit me only once trying to get a carrot slice off my finger- let go before he even hurt me. Idk who left the little guy out there, but they abandoned an absolute sweetheart.

video_title: The best beginner pet lizard
text_content: I got a beardie as my first lizard and yeah, its super annoying. Not the requirements or anything, but the way he looks at me like I owe him rent for living in my own room

video_title: The best beginner pet lizard
text_content: You set that gecko down on the paper and I instantly fell in love. Not a single thought behind those eyes, bless him. Tell him I love him please. 💕

video_title: The best beginner pet lizard
text_content: I will always lean towards beardies personally cause that’s the only lizard I’ve had cuddle with me before 😂 I love a good scaly pupper

video_title: The best beginner pet lizard
text_content: GARGOYLE!!! They make me **so** happy! 😁 A gargoyle is what I have planned to get when the time is right. 🥰

video_title: The best beginner pet lizard
text_content: I agree as a kid I got a beardie and my parents were always annoyed at how much space it would take and the need to take me to the pet store to get crickets and meal worms constantly. A crested gecko would've been so much more simple and easy.

video_title: The best beginner pet lizard
text_content: Gargoyle gecko, crested geckos, or leopard geckos are great beginner species. In my experience, crested geckos are a lot more jumpy, but they are so fun.

video_title: The best beginner pet lizard
text_content:
leopard geckos are also great beginner lizards! my dad bought me one for my tenth birthday because my mom wouldn’t let me get a mammal haha & i had her over 21 years! 

so gentle & sweet, cheap to feed, interactive while also being ok alone & minimal upkeep!

i was even homeless in my early twenties & i took her everywhere, when i had enough money i’d get a motel room & put her in an empty dresser drawer & hunt crickets for her! love/loved her so much ❤️❤️ rip ferocio

video_title: The best beginner pet lizard
text_content: Speaking from experience it’s also not an Iguana lol. I can’t believe my parents let their 10 year old try to keep an iguana in Michigan. RIP Spike I’m sorry you didn’t have a very comfortable life :(

video_title: The best beginner pet lizard
text_content:
So very true about beardies!

Leopard geckos are another great choice
Between them and cresties, it comes down to preference. One has simpler housing requirements and the other has simpler feeding requirements, but they’re both very similar in regards to size and handleability

video_title: The best beginner pet lizard
text_content: I had a bearded dragon as a pet until it awakened its inner spider-man and just started chilling in the ceiling

video_title: The best beginner pet lizard
text_content: I completely agree with this! Crested geckos and gargoyle geckos are the best beginner reptile. I think they are way easier than leopard geckos because leopard geckos need live feeder insects, heat, more space, more humidity, and they tend to be picky eaters. Whereas crested geckos usually do well in the average household temperature, they eat a dried nectar you just mix with water. And they don’t need as much space.

video_title: The best beginner pet lizard
text_content: i love my crestie to bits, he’s so goofy and doesn’t require a ton of effort or money, one $20 bag of food from amazon last me a year, and he lets me hold him whenever

video_title: The best beginner pet lizard
text_content: My first pet lizard experience was a 5 ft green iguana. Space is definitely the hardest part about keeping them. I take care of beardies now too. Beardies are easier to keep but you need to not listen to petco/pet smart for advice on keeping them because they will give you a ton of bad information. video_title: The best beginner pet lizard
text_content: For me, one of the easiest lizards to take care of was a Savannah Monitor. They aren't picky, have iron stomachs and for the most part, very docile. Mine was Bubba, he was about 6-7 when I had to give him to my friend when I moved out of state. Had him a few months after he hatched. When he was young, it was just a diet of insects. Grasshoppers, crickets, roaches, etc. Then when he got bigger, it was usually pinky mice(then full grown mice) and scraps of meat I had leftover while cooking. After the first 2 years, I just let him out in my backyard. He made his home in a den in my garden and was smart enough to not go to the bathroom on the patio and only went in the grass, even though I never potty trained him. He was so sweet and gentle, even with the next door neighbor's 6y/o and all of my friends, even him growing to +6ft long. Under my friend's care, he lived to be 11 years old. Definitely recommend Savs for all reptile enthusiasts. I don't recommend Nile Monitors tho. Those fuckers are mean lol.

Absolutely love this video. Wish it came with every half assed care sheet pet stores hand off for these guys. Wouldn't give mine up for the world, but they (as many other monitors) are not what most people tend to expect with a reptile. All my monitors have rooms, Colonies of food and deep substrate. I gotta tell ya, the cost of the correct substrate as well as the cost of all the larger roach species (well over 1000$ for 8 species of medium and large roaches alone) in the past few months from private breeders, cheaper that way) for food SEVERELY outweighed the cost of converting a bedroom, vet checkups, and continuous electricity for lighting plus the lighting itself altogether. 

Almost all of my monitors, and my Argentine tegu, are adoptees/rescues from people. People who had them in a breeder (40g) tank when they were at least 2 feet long, or loving families who just couldn't take care of them anymore due to either health or housing problems (how I got my Savannahs and my tegu). They were incredibly aggressive, mean, and dangerous animals. But you give them the space they deserve, and the respect they absolutely deserve- they come around so fast it's amazing. My Nile monitor, I got him from someone who said he does nothing but bite and escape. He has the largest room in my enclosure, and does have his days where he doesn't really want to deal with me, but he immediately lost his aggression- he grew curious of me rather than fearful of me. You work on their terms, you give them respect, and in return they give you coexistence. 

Not a lovely dovey animal, definitely dangerous animals  if your not aware of how to interact with them, but incredibly intelligent and absolutely amazing to live with and I love them more than anything in the world. 

Ps. Your also correct, my arms are always scratched to hell. I do have leather handling gloves and cut proof sleeves from work (I work with razor sharp sheet metal, but every cut on me is from one of my animals) but I'm either too lazy or too forgetful to ever put them on. Lucky me I have a high pain tolerance, and but man they make you bleed a LOT.

video_title: Savannah Monitor, The Best Pet Lizard?
video_description: The savannah monitor (Varanus exanthematicus) is one of the coolest animals on planet Earth, but does that make it a good pet?
text_content: I have a savannah monitor... Was nippy when he was younger, but I worked with him. He used to be very cuddly and loved to be held, but now he would rather do his own thing, be it roaming or on a walk around. He is very active, as in, he wants to roam and if you pet him he'll move away or let you do it (which isn't that common anymore). He is very stubborn and will take the path of most resistance. So, what I am trying to say, if you want a cuddly lizard you can really interact with, not a Savannah monitor. It's like owning a scaled cat, you don't do, he does. I still love him, but, he is my first monitor (not my first lizard, I have had iguanas, water dragons and geckos), but I should of started with maybe a tegu or ackie. He does eat well, and I mean he will eat everything. I feed him Raw (food brand) salmon/beef so it's not too fatty but high in nutrients. I love him but he is a grumpy lil guy. His name is Savy because he is savy with the ladies (girls always think he is adorable and want to hold him. He'll let them hold them but literally no men. It's kind of funny, but how he got his name... well also it's short for savannah). Good video, and they are expensive to keep unless you find a big display case ( I have a 8x5x4 and he is 3 1/2 feet), find out that 300+ watt flood lights and ceramic heaters work, and use cypress mulch like it took time for me to find out.

text_content:
I feed my monitor calci worms, horn worms, gut loaded dubias, calcium loaded eggs, few daily gut loaded crickets dusted in calcium, butterworms and wax worms only for his target training and mealworms for snacks. 
  He was an import wild caught young young baby but he was the calmest and FATTEST so I took him in and daily monitor every little thing about him. 
  I own a reptile rescue with 75 permanent resident forever family members "animals" and room for another 160 reacues rehabbers and rehomes.  My savannah monitor is in my top 3 favorite personal animals ever. He is my boy and my doll. Super sweet to handle.  I made sure to keep him warm but not around the action the first 60 days. With multiple small micro insect meals every day by me personally and tiny cameras watching him 24/7 so I could be sure he gets the best start for being a hatchling stressed out. He is now a fancy boy who i adore. Worth every bit of effort. I owe my years of experience especially with high needs rescued animals for the ease of owning my monitor.   I love tegus and other monitors but my savannah is my favorite by far of any lizzard ever.

video_title: Unboxing the second LARGEST lizard in the world‼️🌎🦎
text_content: I worked with monitors for decades. That was a curious tasting bite. He must have thought your neck smelled like it could have been food and he was going in for a taste. That isn’t something that can be trained out by socializing, as it wasn’t an aggressive or defensive bite. I’d never let any large monitor sit on my shoulder or be near my face, but especially a croc monitor. I know that many people put monitors on their shoulders all the time, but it only takes once for something like this to happen that leaves you with horrible and permanent consequences. It’s a miracle that you didn’t get bitten here. I’m glad that you caught this on camera and shared the video, as it is a great opportunity for you and everyone watching to see why they should be careful to not replicate this situation. Croc monitors can be great captives given the right environment and some basic safety procedures. He’s a beautiful healthy looking croc monitor. Thanks for sharing and stay safe.

text_content: Wait, this is news to me. I'm actually very pleasantly surprised that people have figured out captive breeding. Last time I heard about Savannah monitors, the main reason why the consensus was that they don't tend to do well in captivity was because captive breeding hadn't been done successfully at that point. text_content: I had to rescue a baby Savannah Monitor about 15yrs ago when I walked into an apartment and seen a large turtle in a tank so small it could not turn around which I took to my local petstore to save. Above that on the top shelf was a large 3x2ft terrarium but the entire front glass was literally smashed in with the broken glass still. I asked the guy's there what was in there? They laughed and one said there SHOULD be a lizard in there man lol I'm like no possible way? There was nothing but broken glass and a no working dry large waterfall feature...I then looked around that and to my shock there was a tiny 6 inch long Sav Monitor freezing cold to the touch and had not ate in who know's how long. I got very upset and told the guy's there I'm taking the animals and they said they are another guy's they just let him keep them there but he hadn't come back to even feed them. I made them come with me to the pet store to surrender the turtle after they tried to get me to just let it go in the local swamp...And I did not even know what a Sav Monitor was but I was determined to keep him was my first reptile ever...I kept him warm until I got him setup and started feeding him meat...within literal month's he was over 2ft long!! Absolutely amazing and powerful animals!! 👍

video_description: Thank Your Patreon Supporters! You guys make saving these reptiles possible!!! Please join the rescue family!!!
text_content:
I've been saying this my whole life, from what I've seen you really can build a strong bond with most animals on this planet, assuming you were there when they were born, and they have known you for essentially their entire life.

When it comes to exotic reptiles I've only had Bearded Dragons, and I want to get a much bigger species of lizard one day, I just love lizards in particular don't know why, but anyway with my bearded dragons when they ran into a minor health situation like stuck nose shed or clocked femoral pores that did unfortunately require me to squeeze them out.... ( I know you should avoid doing this if possible ) but my male bearded dragon showed no signs of anger, but was uncomfortable obviously. He made no attempts at biting me or even hissing at me. He even still let me pick him up the next day. I really do believe he understood I was helping him, despite how uncomfortable he was, he still shows me signs of complete trust, and amazing thing is this is a bearded dragon I got as an Adult who clearly had 0 human interaction from previous owners as he wanted nothing to do with me, running away from me, puffing beard out at me and even hissing at me sometimes, it took time but he eventually realized I meant him no harm, and now has no issues with me holding him or petting him. I have pictures of before and after trust, but can't post links on Youtube. Animals really are smart, and will quickly gain trust in you, if you have the patience to work with them everyday and show them you mean them no harm.

People also tell me reptiles or in my case Bearded Dragons don't show emotions like a cat or dog does, and I couldn't disagree more, you can tell if they're happy or not. I looked up the signs of stress in bearded dragons and none of my beardys have ever showed signs of stress around me, in fact run to me when they do get scared. No joke decided to take my beardys out on a hot summer day within a few minutes a eagle was flying above my beardy ran to me instead of finding cover. ( I don't anyone to think I'm not looking up in the sky, I do almost every minute this bird must have been hiding in a tree when I brought them out )

text_content: This hasn’t been my experience with savannah monitors at all. My family used to keep them when I was a kid. We had four in total and out of the four, three of them were dog tame. The sweetest, most soft and affectionate lizards you can imagine. The only one who was aggressive was a rescue from an abusive household and even she tamed down to a degree once she was in a safe environment and we’d put in enough time to build her trust. The other three were all so tame that I could handle them as a child. The only times I was ever bitten were during feeding misshapes when our first one was a baby (he’d miss the food and end up biting my fingers) and by our poor girl who came from that awful household I mentioned before. They were the only ones that bit and only one of them bit me on purpose. The one we raised from a hatchling would let go once he realised I wasn’t food and after going through that clumsy phase, I could actually hand feed him with no trouble at all. The other two never bit once.

video_title: What to know before owning a Savannah Monitor! 🦎

text_content: The reason they dig a burrow is to have a humid, cool, safe environment to escape the harsh sun or predators

video_title: What to know before owning a Savannah Monitor! 🦎
video_description: Meet this incredible #savannahmonitorlizard that visited our hospital for a wellness exam! Our #exoticpet doctor, Dr. Patt explains ...
text_content:
hi, doctor patt! i keep savannah monitors. they do not do well in an arid environment…in fact the reason they burrow is not only to escape predators but because the LOVE the moisture beneath the soil. they will develop severe shedding issues if kept in a dry habitat…i have seen this firsthand and am working on rehabilitating a monitor kept like that right now. savannah monitors also have a laterally (vertically) shaped tail which heavily implies swimming abilities…they love moisture, humidity, and water! my boy goes swimming by his own choice very often. moisture also helps their burrows keep their shape. it is very enriching for them, they are not like a bearded dragon. “Immad” made this same point. everything else in this video is accurate. other damaging misconceptions about them: they are insectivores, it is a common belief that they can be fed rats. this causes fatty liver disease, their number one killer.
people underestimate the temperatures that savannah monitors need…while they are usually kept at around 90-100 for their basking spots, they fare much better at 130 degrees and higher. 
people also still believe in infrared lights, which is damaging to their eyes and causes burns. i have seen this firsthand also. it is not effective and they do way better on full-spectrum lighting.

text_content:
This is exactly why most people do not understand the correct way to keep Savannah monitors ! It’s because of Videos like this one giving the wrong information ! Just for the record…I’ve been keeping Savannahs for 5-6yrs now…& that’s 5-6yrs of doing research and taking care of them the correct way ! There is absolutely no reason why any monitors especially Savannahs that are strictly insectivores… Are being fed a grocery store diet… to include ground turkey, chicken gizzards or chicken legs which is what this poor Savannah was being fed in this video…. Which I’m sure is the main reason why it’s overweight  ! Whole pray items are the best to feed monitors & it’s actually cheaper to do so ! Not to mention, way more healthier for the animal ! 
Savannahs are strictly insectivore & in the wild they are mainly eating the large African land snails, millipedes & whatever other insects they can find. If fed the incorrect diet… like grocery store items..Savannahs & other monitors Will develop fatty liver disease pretty quick & we live a very short life 😢😣
If anyone is wanting a Savannah… please do your research first & get the correct care information. Don’t take notes from videos like this ! 
Your Savannah will thank you !

text_content: This is the worst meal to feed Savannahs. That Savannah is super overweight. They are primarily insectivores and can occasionally eat meat. A consistent diet of meat like this savannah is on will inevitably lead to fatty liver disease and it will die a slow painful death. Also, it’s absolutely not good to feed Savannahs any kind of salt water fish or crustaceans, only freshwater fish is acceptable. You are doing a disservice to the public by showing that this is ok, because it is 100% not.

text_content: They are NOT great pets they have extremely difficult requirements for the average person to meet. Extremely hot basking area, UVA/UVB bulbs, a six foot long enclosure with enough space for them to dig, burrow, and climb. They eat insects, but are often fed fatty diets in captivity consisting of mice and meat. They need high protein and ample exercise. They are intelligent, so you have to train them and have the means of keeping them entertained.

video_title: Savannah monitor #livefeeding
text_content: Rodents as a primary food are not a good diet for savannah monitors. It's promotes obesity, since rodents tend to be higher in fat which isn't healthy for reptiles. Insects, large snails, and even small amphibians are a much better diet. Rodents can make for a good treat, but only very rarely, like once or twice a year. It's best to replicate their wild diet as closely as possible. I could also say the same about the enclosure, as it appears to be very small. Savannah monitors require large enclosures.

video_title: Savannah monitor #livefeeding
text_content: Love Savanah monitors and have had them for most of my life. Feeding them high in fat food is a bad idea though because they are prone to fatty liver disease, insects are there main diet. Try hissing cockroaches, it's also very fun to watch them chase them around! 🙂

text_content: I have a one legged Southern Alligator Lizard. I have had her for a few months now. I only saved her because when I saw where it crawled after I thought eh whatever Ill go inside. It went behind a cars tire, I knew it would be only days before I run it over so I brought it in. I have seen it shed once. Shes cool AS HECK. If she had all found legs SHE WOULD RUN AWAY SO BAD. I can't even deal with it with one leg. They are capable of running very fast for their size, and I believe a small aquarium is not suited for them. Those are for geckos and slow moving lizards.

text_content: I caught one baby alligator lizard its in a 10 gallon but I can tell you it does not drink water and gets it moisture from the air or insects. It’s outside no light needed. The tank tends to condense at night plenty of moisture winter time maybe summer put water. It will not eat everyday only eats once every other day or once a week if it eats a big meal. My lizard won’t eat everyday. It’s a wild lizard it doesn’t need lights if it’s outside and you don’t need to feed everyday since in the wild they don’t feed all the time can be hard for them to find food since most insects are fast. Don’t hand it feed and make it lazy make them work for it let them run after it like they do in the wild.

text_content: Pretty good care info, but they do like humidity as they usually live near water.  Southern Alligator Lizards ( Elgaria )  are semi arboreal and like to climb. My baby is in a tall viv and is always up high basking and hunting. You did not mention UVB and they DO need it for bone health and health. They need a low UVB 5% or the new Arcadia arboreal shadedweller kit.  There are breeders and taking an adult from the wild is not such a great thing. They are aggressive and stress out and are probably carrying parasites and when stressed can make them sick. Always take a fecal sample to your reptile vet to make sure.  Babies are delicate but hardy and will take to being handled much better. Keep temps on the lower side even for the basking area. They can dehydrate easy especially babies.  I had one for 15 years and this is my second one. I also have its Mexican cousin ,  A Mexican Alligator lizard ( Abronia Graminae)  Bright green with yellow eyes and have had her for over a year.

video_title: I Recommend Pet Crocodiles FOR BEGINNERS! The Safest "Crocodilian" Pets!
video_description: Alligators and Crocodiles make TERRIBLE Pets for most reptile keepers right? These 5 Crocodilian pets make amazing pets even ...
text_content:
I have a crocodile gecko that I caught in my bedroom (they're common in my country, Portugal). They are very shy of human contact, specially if they're wild caught but they're so cute and cool looking. They're small and don't require a lot of care like other lizards, they're pretty hardy too. They just need a vertical terrarium cause they like to climb and also having caves/rocks/trunks on the floor so they can hide. Mine spends all day hidden inside a little cave and comes out at sunset/night. Sometimes she's out during the day but it's rare. They can drop their tail too and regrow it but it won't look as good as the original. From the experience I have with my crocodile gecko she won't drop her tail so easily, I've handled her while cleaning the terrarium and and when I moved her from terrarium and I could tell she was scared and stressed out cause of my presence and touch but she never dropped her tail which I think is a good sign. Even then, they'll often hide very quickly if they see people even from afar.
Ps: this is my experience with a wild caught and doesn't mean it applies to other geckos from the same species, specially if they're bred in captivity but I hope it helps. Also she was still a juvenile when caught and so far (2 years later), luckily, she's doing fine with no appearant issues.

video_title: Feeding my caiman !
text_content: Nigel and Cheesecake, along with their other pond friends, have lived together for a long time. You’ll notice many zoos, reptariums, aquariums etc. also house turtles and crocodilians/gators together, taking into consideration the size of the habitat and the species/behaviors of all animals involved. Since it is not ideal to keep more than one caiman in an enclosure and additional considerations need to be made in order to do so, quick turtles and large fish make ideal cohab candidates. Adding multiple species helps create an enriching environment, similar to their natural habitat. Without these animals, Nigel could potentially become depressed and (dare I say it for the new grads in the back,) even lonely. Although he snaps at Cheesecake’s overbearing and in-your-face behavior during snack time, it’s out of frustration, like “get out of the way damnit” rather than “I’m hungry and I want to eat you.” Nigel certainly could hurt Cheesecake during one of these interactions but it is highly unlikely as cheesecake is simply too fast for him and has years of “experience” dodging his annoyed snaps. Nigel lives with turtles, large slow fish and one little fish. All are healthy and happy. 👌

video_title: Ball Pythons Are NOT The Best Beginner PET Snake! | THIS IS!
video_description: Most people automatically recommend ball pythons as the best beginner snake. But after years of keeping both species, many ...
text_content:
My first snake's a rosy boa and he's wonderful; docile just like his rainforest relative, he eats readily, and every time I take him out he's enthusiastically exploring. But I also need to state that I'm in Canada where rosy boas are *extremely difficult to find because almost nobody breeds them here* and that it took me years to find him. They are RARE snakes here! Here in Canada, there's *nothing* common about my little stripey boy!

Meanwhile, Boa imperator is stupidly illegal in my province, so I couldn't adopt Churro's big cousin if I wanted to. And I didn't want to because right now I still find the scale of even adult ball pythons intimidating. Meanwhile I can scoop up Churro one handed, and because he's smaller, it's easier for me to give him an epic enclosure that is much bigger than his species' stated minimum.

text_content: you can tell the gender by the thickness of the tail! after the vent (the hole) on your snakes belly if the width is the same as the body and slowly tapers its a female, if it turns skinny fast (smaller than the width of the snake) it is male :] this can be hard to tell sometimes also depending on age so the other methods are probing and popping out hemipeens (if there are any, its a male) but those two methods are better adviced to trained professionals and vets as it is a delicate process and can cause injury or prolapse which is not good for our noodle friends! hope this helped :]

text_content: Hello Peoples, I'm currently watching this video and wanted to add some info on salmonella.  I have 3 snakes currently and have never got Salmonella from them.  However I used to volunteer at a reptile center which had hundreds of animals and it was almost like a sauna there sometimes. My first week I was helping to feed snakes and was sweating profusely. As I wiped my face with my hands which had some spilled water on them from carrying a container full of rats in warm water. Some of my sweat went into my mouth and I got Salmonella.   I had a severe case twice because the first round of antibiotics didn't eradicate the salmonella from my body.  So Christmas day (of course) it came back with a vengeance and I ended up in the hospital.   Anyways, I likely got it worse as I take stomach acid suppressing medication which allows the bug to proliferate more.  Point being is to be more careful handling rats and snake food then the snakes themselves.  Also most people who get salmonella usually experience a stomach bug that's not too serious.   I believe my medication made me far more vulnerable to having a severe case.  I hope this helps some of you who are concerned.  Either way it's always a good idea to wash your hands afterwards.  For snakes, salmonella is found in there fecal matter and has no effect on the snakes themselves 😊

text_content:
For me it seems to be a myth that snakes only can digest food well if they are sitting on their hotspot.
I have had my ball python for three years , she is totally healthy (said my snake specialist vet) , eats well, is at a weight of 1600 gram and she never ever digests on her hotspot.
When she was a baby she spent a lot of time on her hot spot but after like 6 months she started to visit it less and less.
She always digests her meals at a spot with a temperature of 25 to 26 degrees Celsius / 77 to 78.8 degrees.
First I was really worried.
But as she is totally fine I got used to it.
My cornsnakes don't use their hotspots either for digesting.
