text_content: As far as target or tap training. I went a whole different direction entirely. Although I only have ball pythons so I have no idea how well this would work with other snakes. But I found snakes understand routine. I always feed mine after handling. Never before. So every feeding I take mine out let em get some exercise and then when they go back into the enclosure they get food.... like I said, I have no idea if this would work on a more food motivated species. But it's worked well with ball pythons.

text_content:
✨️I think you should go partial, if not full-on, bioactive setup with Justina's current monster pen. Its current state serves its purpose for her, but that's just about it. It could be so much more with the addition of just a little more life to it: a living, partially mixed (⅔ topsoil & ⅓ play sand) substrate, with a small number of initial clean-up crew members (earthworms, pill bug isopods, & some springtails), some live plants indigenous to the King Cobra's birthplace region(s) that will do well in indoor living conditions. A more natural lighting setup that's beneficial for the snake's warmth, as well as the live plants' daily artificial sunshine requirements. And, most importantly, an indoor automatic misting sprinkler system/sprayer(s) built directly into the enclosure itself. How sweet would 2-3 brief mist-outs look, as well as physically benefit Justina's skin throughout her shedding processes? The added moisture will also create additional humidity-controlled atmospheric conditions indigenous to her native geography, nearly year-round.
Some multi-tiered planks of lumber or thick-cut branches from a fallen tree would definitely add enrichment. Along with an additional soaking water feature or filtered tub, a bare-bones setup, and maybe a hollowed-out log with some scattered stonework would tie everything together. I'm sorry about Kevin, but I feel that was a freak incident and had nothing to do with anything I'm referring to—as in incorporating into Justina's barren wasteland
(no offense...👒💨➰️👌🏻⛳️).✨️

text_content:
I HAVE TEA!!



So, I recently got my new pet snake, Espresso, and everything was fine (I thought), until I went upstairs to my room to check on him (that's where his enclosure is). It was like those moments where you didn't really think about doing something, it was just a little push that sent you up. So I went to check on him, and he wasn't under the stone hide that he had been in for the last couple of days, so I checked the other things, took everything out even, but he wasn't there. (BIG HEADS UP: we left a small opening as wide as a fingernail to let some humidity out of the enclosure because we were told too much humidity had a chance to cause infections). I turned my entire room upside down until my dad found him under a pile of clothes and bags in a corner. I should've known he'd be there, he loves dark spaces.

text_content: I held one of these snakes on my shoulder and around my neck (exact same color) at a cub scout meeting where a wildlife guy came to show animals to the boys.  He asked if anyone wanted a pic with the snake and I volunteered.  I had to shout at my son to come quick because the snake was so heavy that I was not sure how much longer I could hold it and I'm not a weakling.  Snake was calm and a magnificent animal used to being picked up, I estimate was 8-10 feet long.  Having said that, these pythons have been released into our Florida everglades and as a powerful invasive predator, they cause great harm to the native wildlife.

text_content:
Celebensis owner here - he's a VERY good boy and it is ALL ABOUT THE ENCLOSURE. We had to keep in the bathroom for a week for some enclosure updates and he was a NIGHTMARE. Whips. Running - still wouldn't bite. But he was PISSED.
The minute we got him back in his $20,000 enclosure (I only say this to scare off unprepared buyers) he instantly went right back to normal. Calm. Curious. Gentle. I feed him with tongs, but he gets qual eggs by hand as treats.
Definitely not for beginners - the expertise and cost of the enclosure is massive, but he's worth it.

video_title: Meet me and the animals! #savannahmonitor #monitorlizard #biglizard #training #socialization #cute
text_content:
Hey there ! Your video was the 1st to pop up & I saw Savannah monitor so I had to watch ! I have to share this with you….your story is EXACTLY like mine ! No joke ! 
My 1st monitor…
My 1st ever reptile was a Savannah monitor named Joanna ! I called her Joanna Savannah 🥰 
& just like Jabba….Joannah was my world ! I got her as a baby….she literally fit in the palm of my hand ! I had her for a little over four years & she got to be almost 2 1/2 feet. But very sadly & very unexpectedly she passed away December of 2023. She literally took her last breath on me as I was holding her up on my shoulder outside in the sunshine where she loved to be ! I had a special plaque made with her footprints on it, and I have another one made with her picture on it. I started my YouTube channel a few years ago because of her ! It still breaks my heart that she’s not around so I can totally relate to you big time ! 
Today I do have another Savannah named JoJo. She’s a character ! I absolutely love Savannahs and will always have them in my life ! 
If you ever produce CBB Savannahs let me know ! 
It’s funny… I also have an Ackie monitor & I used to have two white throats in the past. 
I also have an adult male peach throat monitor ! I got him as a baby & he’s about 3 1/2 years old now. He’s my world ! He’s super friendly and very tame and social ! The bond that him and I have is absolutely incredible !
I noticed your grow tent in the background…. I have my peach throat in one and I think they are amazing for reptile enclosures ! 
I also have 3 leopard geckos & 1 gargoyle gecko as well. 
Didn’t mean to write a book here lol. But wanted to share all of that with you because I see that you are just as passionate about your reptiles as I am !
